title: Blueprint — The Cursor/Context-Aware Desktop Agent
summary: The capstone: a complete end-to-end design for a context- and platform-aware desktop assistant that senses your screen, app state, and mouse cursor, then figures out where to go and what to search to act on your behalf — built by applying the whole framework.
analogy: Imagine the world's best executive assistant sitting beside you: they watch where you're looking and what you're pointing at, quietly understand the app you're in, and the instant you hesitate they hand you exactly the right thing — but they never send an email in your name without a nod first.
body:
## Problem statement
We want a 'masterpiece' desktop agent that is **context-aware** (it understands what the user is doing right now) and **platform-aware** (it understands the OS and the active app's capabilities). It senses the **screen, app state, and mouse cursor**, infers intent, and then **knows where to go and what to search** to act on the user's behalf — surface the right document, run the right command, fill the right field — while staying private and safe. We design it by walking the six framework steps end-to-end.

## Step 1 — Requirements
**Who:** a desktop power user (developer, analyst, writer) on macOS/Windows, working across many native apps and the browser.

**Functional requirements**
- Sense cursor position, hover/selection, the active app + window, and on demand the screen.
- Infer the user's likely intent (define, summarize, fix, find, navigate, fill).
- Propose the single most useful next action, anchored at the cursor.
- Ground that action to a concrete UI target and/or a search query.
- Execute the action (search, open, click, type, call an API) — after the right level of confirmation.
- Remember user preferences and accepted/rejected suggestions.

**Non-functional requirements**
- **Latency:** cursor-anchored cue ≤100 ms; full suggestion ~1 s; any multi-step action streams progress (NN/g limits).
- **Autonomy:** V1 is *confirm-then-act* for anything that writes; *suggest-only* glow for read actions.
- **Privacy:** screen contents and keystrokes never leave the device un-redacted; secure input fields are never captured.
- **Safety:** no irreversible action (send, delete, pay) without explicit confirmation; on-screen text is untrusted.
- **Success metric:** ≥60% suggestion acceptance, median assisted task <30 s, near-zero privacy incidents.
- **Scope (V1):** one suggestion at a time; act within the active app + a search panel; no cross-app automation chains yet.

## Step 2 — Constraints & budgets
Decompose the ~1 s suggestion budget: **sense ≤150 ms** (read a11y tree + cursor; OCR only on demand) → **ground ≤150 ms** (rank + compress on-device) → **plan ~600 ms** (cloud LLM call) → **guardrail+act ≤100 ms** → **render ≤50 ms**. The ≤100 ms cursor glow is **on-device only** — no network. Context budget: aim for ~4–8k tokens of *ranked* context, never the raw capture. Cost: ~1–3 model calls per accepted task; a small on-device model ranks context and gates privacy so the expensive cloud call fires only when needed.

## Step 3 — High-level design
The agent instantiates the reference architecture. Signal sources feed a Context Engine; a Decision Engine (planner) maps context to an action, a UI target, and a search query; guardrails gate execution; an Action layer drives the OS; Memory personalizes; the UI is a cursor-anchored overlay plus a side panel. See the architecture diagram.

## The signal sources (platform sensing)
- **Cursor position + velocity** — where attention is; a slow-down/hover triggers sensing.
- **Hover / selection** — the exact element or text in focus (the strongest intent signal).
- **Active app via accessibility tree** — structured roles/names/bounds of on-screen elements; the cheap, stable backbone.
- **Screen via screenshot + OCR** — fallback for canvas/image apps with no a11y data, captured on demand only.
- **History** — recent actions, clipboard, recently opened files.
- **OS capabilities** — what the platform/app can do: available menu commands, deep-link/URL schemes, automation APIs (AppleScript/UIA), installed apps. This is the **platform-awareness** that tells the agent *where it can go*.

## The Decision Engine — 'where to go and what to search'
Given the compressed context, the planner runs a constrained ReAct-style decision:
1. **Classify intent** from the focused element + history (define / fix / find / summarize / navigate / fill).
2. **Choose a destination** using OS capabilities: a menu command, an app deep-link, a settings pane, or a web/file search. (e.g. selected error → open the IDE Quick-Fix; selected name → open contact card; ambiguous → search.)
3. **Compose the query** from the selection + surrounding context (the precise string to look up).
4. **Emit a typed action** `{tool, target(a11y selector), query, confidence}` — never raw pixels, never free-form.
The planner is constrained to the live tool schema and validated targets, so it can't hallucinate a destination that doesn't exist.

## The action / automation layer
Typed tools with permissions: `search(query)`, `open(app|url|file)`, `runCommand(menuPath)`, `click(target)`, `type(text)`, `callApi(name,args)`. Targets are resolved against the live a11y tree at execution time (re-validate before acting). Platform adapters wrap OS automation (macOS Accessibility/AppleScript, Windows UI Automation) behind one interface.

## Guardrails + human-in-the-loop
- **Permission tiers:** read actions auto-run; write/click actions need a one-key confirm; irreversible actions (send/delete/pay) need explicit confirm with a preview.
- **Target re-validation:** confirm the selector still matches before clicking; abort if confidence dropped.
- **Injection defense:** on-screen text is data, not instructions; tools are sandboxed to the active app's permitted scope.
- **Budgets:** step/iteration cap and a per-task token/time budget to prevent loops.
- **Undo:** prefer reversible actions; expose an immediate undo.

## Privacy / on-device strategy
A two-tier split: an **on-device small model + heuristics** handle capture, ranking, redaction, and the instant cursor cue — none of this leaves the machine. Only **redacted, compressed context** is sent to the cloud planner, and only when a cloud-grade decision is needed. Secure input fields (password/credit-card roles in the a11y tree) are never captured. The user sees a clear capture indicator and can pause sensing at any time. (Full threat model in the sensing-and-privacy blueprint.)

## Step 5 — Trade-offs (chosen on purpose)
| Axis | Choice (V1) | What we gain | What we give up |
|---|---|---|---|
| Autonomy | Confirm-then-act for writes | Safety, reversibility, trust | Some speed / fewer hands-free wins |
| On-device vs cloud | Split: sense+redact local, plan cloud | Privacy + ≤100ms cues + capable planning | More engineering complexity |
| Sensing source | a11y tree first, OCR on demand | Low latency, stable targets, less data | Misses pure-canvas apps until OCR fires |
| Targeting | a11y selectors, not pixels | Survives re-layout/resolution | Needs per-platform adapters |
| Context size | ~4–8k ranked tokens | Fast, cheap, smaller privacy surface | Occasional missed context → ask user |
| Proactivity | One suggestion, cursor-anchored | Helpful, low annoyance | Less ambient automation |

## Step 6 — Review (walk one request)
User selects a red error in VS Code → cursor slows → **sense** reads a11y (role=staticText, the error text) in ~120 ms and shows an instant glow → **ground** sets intent=fix, query='TypeError undefined is not a function', target=Quick-Fix → **plan** (cloud) confirms the best action in ~600 ms → **guardrail** sees a read/navigate action, auto-allowed → **act** opens the Quick-Fix panel and offers a web search → **memory** records acceptance → UI streams the result. Total ~1 s, one optional click, nothing private left the device. Every Step-1 requirement is met.

## Where it goes next (V2+)
Cross-app automation chains, multi-step plans with a planner-executor, learned per-user shortcuts from memory, and proactive (act-then-report) autonomy for low-risk, high-confidence actions only.
diagram:
flowchart TD
  subgraph Device [On-device · private · ≤150ms]
    direction TB
    subgraph Sensors
      CUR[Cursor x,y + velocity]
      SEL[Hover / selection]
      A11Y[Active app a11y tree]
      OCR[Screenshot + OCR on-demand]
      HIS[History / clipboard]
      CAP[OS capabilities\nmenus · deep-links · automation APIs]
    end
    CE[Context Engine\nnormalize · rank · compress]
    RED[[Redaction + secure-field filter]]
    SM[Small on-device model\nrank + instant cursor cue]
    CUR & SEL & A11Y & OCR & HIS & CAP --> CE --> RED --> SM
    MEM[(Memory\nprefs · accepted/rejected · shortcuts)]
    MEM <--> CE
  end
  subgraph Cloud [Cloud · only redacted context]
    PLAN[Decision Engine / Planner\n1 classify intent\n2 choose destination via OS caps\n3 compose query\n4 emit typed action]
  end
  SM -- redacted, compressed context --> PLAN
  PLAN -- '{tool, target(a11y), query, confidence}' --> GR
  subgraph Safety
    GR{{Guardrails\npermission tier · re-validate target · injection defense · budgets}}
  end
  GR -- read: auto --> ACT
  GR -- write/irreversible: confirm --> HITL[Human-in-the-loop\nconfirm + preview + undo]
  HITL --> ACT
  subgraph Actions
    ACT[Action layer\nsearch · open · runCommand · click · type · callApi]
    ADP[Platform adapters\nmacOS AX/AppleScript · Windows UIA]
    ACT --> ADP
  end
  ADP --> OSW[(OS / active app)]
  ACT -- result --> MEM
  ADP --> UI[UI overlay\ncursor-anchored hint · side panel · streaming]
  UI -. next signal .-> CUR
  UI -- feedback --> MEM

examples.what: A productized screen-sensing agent that perceives a UI, plans the next action, and clicks/types — pausing for user confirmation on sensitive steps; the closest public analog to this blueprint's loop and guardrails.
A model that takes screenshots and emits clicks/keystrokes to operate a computer — validates the perceive→ground→act path and the need for tight safety scoping.
Frames agents as planner + tools + memory in an environment; this blueprint is that abstraction grounded into a concrete desktop, cursor-aware product.

drills.q: How does this agent decide 'where to go' for a given intent?
drills.a: The Decision Engine classifies intent, then chooses a destination from the platform's OS capabilities — a menu command, app deep-link, settings pane, or web/file search — constrained to destinations that actually exist on the system.

drills.q: Why does the cursor glow run on-device while planning runs in the cloud?
drills.a: The ≤100 ms 'instant' perception cue has no time for a network round-trip, and keeping capture local protects privacy; only redacted, compressed context is sent to the more capable cloud planner.

drills.q: What stops the planner from clicking a stale or wrong element?
drills.a: Actions are typed with a11y-selector targets and a confidence score; guardrails re-validate the selector against the live a11y tree right before execution and abort or ask if confidence dropped.

teachItBack: This capstone designs a desktop assistant that senses your cursor, selection, active app via the accessibility tree, and screen, compresses that into ranked context on-device, and sends only redacted context to a cloud planner that decides where to go (using the OS's real capabilities) and what to search. Typed actions with accessibility-selector targets are gated by tiered guardrails — read actions auto-run, writes need a confirm, irreversible actions need a preview — so it acts on your behalf without acting recklessly. It's the whole framework applied: requirements, perception budgets, a seven-block architecture, a grounding deep-dive, and a deliberate trade-off table, all verified by walking one request end-to-end in about a second.

sources.title: NN/g — Response Times: The 3 Important Limits

glossary.definition: An agent that infers what the user is doing right now from live signals (cursor, selection, screen, app state) and adapts its actions accordingly.
An agent that knows the OS and active app's capabilities — menus, deep-links, automation APIs — so it knows where it can actually go to act.
A structured, schema-constrained command {tool, target, query, confidence} the agent emits instead of free-form text or raw pixel clicks, preventing hallucinated or unstable actions.

quiz.q: In this blueprint, what gives the agent its platform-awareness — the sense of where it can go to act?
choices: The OS capabilities source: available menus, deep-links, and automation APIs
quiz.explanation: Knowing the platform/app's real capabilities lets the Decision Engine pick destinations that actually exist.

quiz.q: Why are action targets expressed as accessibility selectors rather than pixel coordinates?
choices: a11y selectors survive layout, resolution, and theme changes so actions stay correct
quiz.explanation: Stable a11y selectors keep the agent pointed at the right element even when the UI shifts, unlike brittle pixel coordinates.
